contentBlocks.title: Why convert JSON to XML?
contentBlocks.html:
JSON is the standard for REST APIs and modern web apps. However, many enterprise systems, SOAP APIs, B2B data exchange standards (EDI, UBL, e-invoicing), and configuration files require XML. Converting JSON to XML bridges the gap between modern and legacy systems.

XML supports schema validation (XSD), namespaces, XSLT transformations, and digital signatures — features JSON lacks. In the enterprise world, XML remains the standard for SAP, Oracle, banking APIs (ISO 20022), and government systems.

The converter maps JSON objects to XML elements, arrays to repeating elements, and primitive types to text values. It generates well-formed, indented XML with a proper <?xml version="1.0"?> declaration.

All processing happens locally in your browser — nothing is sent to any server. No registration, no limits, no tracking.

items.title: Paste your data

contentBlocks.title: How does JSON to XML conversion work?
contentBlocks.html:
JSON and XML are both structured data formats, but they use fundamentally different syntax. JSON uses curly braces and key-value pairs, while XML uses opening and closing tags to define elements. The converter maps each JSON property to an XML element, preserving the hierarchical structure.

JSON objects become XML elements, JSON arrays become repeated elements, and primitive values (strings, numbers, booleans) become text content within their parent tags. The converter generates well-formed XML with proper indentation and an XML declaration header.

XML is widely used in enterprise systems, SOAP web services, configuration files, and data interchange where strict schema validation is required. The conversion runs entirely in your browser — no data is sent to any server.

contentBlocks.title: Tips for converting JSON to XML
contentBlocks.description: A few tips to help you avoid common issues during conversion:

items.title: Root element
items.description: XML requires a single root element. If your JSON is an array, the converter wraps it in a root element. You can customize the root element name if needed.

items.title: Array handling
items.description: JSON arrays are converted to repeated XML elements with the same tag name. The element name is derived from the parent key or uses a generic item tag.

items.title: Special characters
items.description: Characters like <, >, &, and quotes are automatically escaped in the XML output using standard XML entities to ensure valid markup.

items.title: Attributes vs elements
items.description: By default, all JSON properties become XML child elements. JSON does not have a native concept of XML attributes, so no attributes are generated.

contentBlocks.title: JSON to XML mapping rules
contentBlocks.html:
The converter maps JSON structures to XML elements following these rules:

  • Objects – each key becomes an XML element. {"name": "John"} → <name>John</name>
  • Arrays – each item becomes a repeating element with the key name. {"items": [1, 2]} → <items>1</items><items>2</items>
  • Primitives – strings, numbers, booleans become text content of their parent element.
  • Null values – converted to empty elements or omitted.

The output includes a proper XML declaration and uses 2-space indentation for readability. Element names are derived directly from JSON keys.

contentBlocks.title: Frequently asked questions about JSON to XML conversion

items.question: Is converting JSON to XML free?
items.answer: Yes, this converter is completely free with no limits. No registration, no watermarks.

items.question: Does the converter upload my files?
items.answer: No. All processing happens locally in your browser. Your files never leave your device.

items.question: Can I convert multiple files at once?
items.answer: Yes, the converter supports batch conversion. Add multiple files and convert them all simultaneously.

items.question: Does the converter work on mobile?
items.answer: Yes, the converter works on any device with a modern web browser, including smartphones and tablets.

items.question: Is converting JSON to XML safe?
items.answer: Yes. The entire conversion runs locally in your browser. Your data is never sent to any server. When you close the page, all data is automatically cleared from memory.

items.question: Is the conversion lossless?
items.answer: Yes. All data from the JSON input is preserved in the XML output. The structure and values remain intact, though the syntax changes from JSON to XML notation.

items.question: Can I validate the XML output?
items.answer: The converter produces well-formed XML that passes standard XML validation. If you need schema validation (XSD), you can apply your schema to the output separately.

items.question: Can I use the XML output in Salesforce or SAP integrations?
items.answer: Yes. Both Salesforce and SAP commonly exchange data in XML format. The converter produces well-formed XML with a proper declaration that you can use as a data import payload, SOAP request body, or file attachment in these enterprise systems.
